text: Hello everyone, welcome to another video presentation on laparoscopic colorectal surgery. Today we are going to show you our technique of complete laparoscopic sigmoid and rectal mobilization in a male patient.

The surgeon incises the peritoneum over the aorta and continues the incision cranially until reaching the likely site of origin of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA). The IMA is then divided approximately three centimeters above its origin, taking care to avoid damage to the surrounding sympathetic plexus.

The surgeon identifies and divides the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) located lateral to the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA). The dissection plane is posterior to the divided vessels, revealing the left ureter and gonadal vessels beneath the plane of medial to lateral mesentery dissection.

The surgeon reaches the posterior dissection plane of the mesorectum, carefully avoiding the aortic bifurcation and sacral promontory to protect the superior hypogastric plexus. The dissection proceeds in the avascular plane, extending caudally while avoiding monopolar diathermy to prevent damage to pelvic veins and potential bleeding.

The surgeon releases the sigmoid colon and left colon from their lateral pelvic attachments, achieving complete mobilization of the lower left colon. The sigmoid colon is divided using an endostapler, facilitating pelvic dissection.

The surgeon incises the peritoneal fold anterior to the recto-vesical pouch, preparing for further pelvic dissection. The lateral dissection is continued deep into the pelvis, taking care to avoid the right and left hypogastric nerves and the inferior hypogastric plexus.

The surgeon performs anterior dissection, revealing the seminal vesicles. The dissection proceeds through the thin but consistent plane of Denonvilliers' fascia, extending towards the base of the prostate.
The surgeon mobilizes the lowermost rectum by dividing the fascial attachments to the lateral and posterior pelvic wall, revealing the pelvic floor. The rectum is visibly separated from the prostate.

After completing the dissection, the muscular and rectal junction is visible, devoid of fat. The specimen is delivered perineally, revealing the glistening bilobed appearance of the mesorectum posterior to the rectum.
